request.question: What is the setting for the game engine Snowdrop?

title: List of game engine recreations
Game engine
Id Tech 4
Unreal Engine
Rockstar Advanced Game Engine
Amazon Lumberyard
Snowdrop (game engine)
Game engine recreation
Tom Clancy's The Division
Zero (game engine)
sentences: Game engine recreation is a denomination for game engines that are clones of the original game engine binary that came with the video games.
The main motivation behind such a project is the wish to play the game on other operating systems than those supported by the original game engine.
A game engine is a software framework designed for the creation and development of video games. Developers use them to create games for consoles, mobile devices and personal computers.

The core functionality typically provided by a game engine includes a rendering engine ("renderer") for 2D or 3D graphics, a physics engine or collision detection (and collision response), sound, scripting, animation, artificial intelligence, networking, streaming, memory management, threading, localization support, scene graph, and may include video support for cinematics.
The process of game development is often economized, in large part, by reusing/adapting the same game engine to create different games, or to make it easier to port games to multiple platforms.
id Tech 4, popularly known as the "Doom 3" engine, is a game engine developed by id Software and first used in the video game "Doom 3".
The engine was designed by John Carmack.
Carmack also created previous game engines, such as those for Doom and Quake, which are widely recognized as significant advances in the field.
This OpenGL-based game engine has also been used in "Quake 4", "Prey", "", "Wolfenstein", and "Brink".
The Unreal Engine is a game engine developed by Epic Games, first showcased in the 1998 first-person shooter game "Unreal".

Although primarily developed for first-person shooters, it has been successfully used in a variety of other genres, including stealth, MMORPGs, and other RPGs. With its code written in C++, the Unreal Engine features a high degree of portability and is a tool used by many game developers today.
It has won several awards, including the Guinness World Records award for "most successful video game engine."
The Rockstar Advanced Game Engine (RAGE) is a proprietary game engine developed by the RAGE Technology Group at Rockstar San Diego, with contributions by other Rockstar Games subsidiaries.
The engine has been used on several different platforms such as Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3, PlayStation 4, Wii, Xbox 360 and Xbox One.
The Angel Game Engine was originally developed by Angel Studios for "Midtown Madness" and later the sixth generation console era versions of the "Midnight Club" series and other Angel Studios games.
Amazon Lumberyard is a free cross-platform triple-A game engine developed by Amazon and based on the architecture of CryEngine, which was licensed from Crytek in 2015.
The engine features integration with Amazon Web Services to allow developers to build or host their games on Amazon's servers, as well as support for livestreaming via Twitch.

Additionally, the engine includes Twitch ChatPlay, where viewers of the Twitch stream can influence the game through the associated chat, a method of play inspired by the "Twitch Plays Pokémon" phenomenon.
The source code is available to end users with limitations: users may not publicly release the Lumberyard engine source code or use it to release their own game engine.

Lumberyard launched on February 9, 2016 alongside GameLift, a fee-based managed service for deploying and hosting multiplayer games, intended to allow developers to easily develop games that attract "large and vibrant communities of fans."

s of February 2017 , the software is currently in beta and can be used to build games for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4, Xbox One, with limited support for iOS and Android, and support for Linux and Mac coming as well.
Virtual reality integration was added in Beta 1.3, allowing developers to build games supporting devices like Oculus Rift and HTC Vive.
Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.
It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.
Game engine recreations are remade engine interpreters for video games that replace the original engine binary that came with the original game.

A notable example of game engine recreation is ScummVM which successfully recreated the SCUMM engine of classical LucasArts' point and click adventures. For further examples, refer to the list of game engine recreations.

Tom Clancy's The Division is an online-only action role-playing video game developed by Massive Entertainment and published by Ubisoft, with assistance from Red Storm Entertainment, for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4 and Xbox One.
It was announced during Ubisoft's E3 2013 press conference, and was released worldwide on 8 March 2016.

It is set in a near future New York City in the aftermath of a smallpox pandemic; the player, who is an agent of the eponymous Strategic Homeland Division, commonly referred to as simply "The Division", is tasked with helping the group rebuild its operations in Manhattan, investigate the nature of the outbreak, and combating criminal activity in its wake.
"The Division" is structured with elements of role-playing games, as well as collaborative and player versus player online multiplayer.
Zero is a proprietary game engine created by Pandemic Studios.
It was used first in the game "" and later used in several "Star Wars" games including the popular "Battlefront" series.
"Battlezone II: Combat Commander" and "Dark Reign 2" both feature an in-engine editor accessible via commands.
The engine was revamped for "" to accommodate consoles and third person gameplay.
The engine was again retooled for "" and the level editor was made a separate entity from the game engine.
A set of modding tools including ZeroEdit, the new level creation tool, were released for use with "Star Wars: Battlefront" on December 23, 2004.
An updated version of the tools were released on February 2, 2006 for "".
Pandemic used Zero as their primary engine for several of their games developed in their Los Angeles, California studio.
A modified version of the Zero engine was also used in Pandemic's "Mercenaries 2: World in Flames".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What is the setting for the game engine Snowdrop?
hop1_docs: Snowdrop (game engine) | Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.  It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.

Information set (game theory) | In game theory, an information set is a set that, for a particular player, establishes all the possible moves that could have taken place in the game so far, given what that player has observed.  If the game has perfect information, every information set contains only one member, namely the point actually reached at that stage of the game.  Otherwise, it is the case that some players cannot be sure exactly what has taken place so far in the game and what their position is.

List of snowdrop gardens | Snowdrops (species of "Galanthus") are popular late winter or early spring flowers which are celebrated as a sign of spring and can attract large numbers of visitors to places where they are growing.  The reason for their popularity is that snowdrops can form impressive carpets of white in areas where they are native or have been naturalised.  Most 'Snowdrop Gardens' will have the common snowdrop, "Galanthus nivalis", but some have more unusual snowdrops, some which may be unique to the garden that they are growing in.  The rarest may only survive in that garden due to the conditions and environment.
HMS Snowdrop | Two ships of the Royal Navy have borne the name HMS "Snowdrop" after the flower, the Snowdrop:

Galanthus nivalis | Galanthus nivalis, the snowdrop or common snowdrop, is the best-known and most widespread of the 20 species in its genus, "Galanthus".  Snowdrops are among the first bulbs to bloom in spring and can form impressive carpets of white in areas where they are native or have been naturalised.  They should not be confused with the snowflakes, in the genera "Leucojum" and "Acis".

USS Snowdrop (1863) | USS "Snowdrop", originally named "Albert DeGroat" and occasionally referred to as "A. DeGroat" was a screw tug built in 1863 at Buffalo, New York.  "Albert DeGroat" was purchased by the United States Navy at New York City on 16 October 1863; renamed "Snowdrop"; and was fitted out at the New York Navy Yard.
HMS Snowdrop (1915) | HMS "Snowdrop" was an "Azalea"-class sloop of the Royal Navy.
response.summary_1:
- Facts from passages:
  - [1] "Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft" (quote).
  - [1] "for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One." (quote).
  - [1] "first revealed at E3 2013 with 'Tom Clancy's The Division'" (quote).
  - [3],[4],[5],[6],[7] Other unrelated "Snowdrop" pages (flowers, ships) exist in passages (paraphrase).

- Bridge entities and ambiguities:
  - Bridge entity: Snowdrop (game engine) — present and unambiguous in [1].
  - Bridge entity: Tom Clancy's The Division — referenced in [1] but its page/content is not included in passages.
  - Ambiguity: the word "setting" is unclear — could mean (A) the engine's runtime/platform/environment (a technical "setting") or (B) the fictional setting/location/time of a game that uses the engine (e.g., The Division). This ambiguity prevents answering definitively.

- Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve ambiguity (fetch these pages/sections):
  1. Fetch "Snowdrop (game engine)" full page — extract infobox fields and sections: "Platforms", "Operating systems", "Supported platforms", "Development/Design goals", and "Games using Snowdrop"/"Notable uses".
  2. If intent may be the game's fictional setting: fetch "Tom Clancy's The Division" page — extract "Setting", "Plot", or infobox "Setting"/"Location" to get the game's setting (location/time).
  3. If user meant a different "Snowdrop" (flower/ship), fetch the relevant disambiguation or that specific page (e.g., "Galanthus nivalis", "HMS Snowdrop") and extract the descriptive "Setting" or context field as applicable.

- Do not answer yet: required fact (which sense of "setting" is intended and, if the game's setting is requested, the Division's setting) is missing or ambiguous in the supplied passages.
response.hop2_query: Snowdrop (game engine) platforms infobox supported platforms
hop2_docs: Leadwerks | Leadwerks is a cross-platform game engine developed by Leadwerks Software that focuses on ease of use and learning.  The software supports Windows and Linux, with OS X support in development.  Leadwerks is currently on its fourth major version and is sold through the Steam digital distribution platform.

Snowdrop (game engine) | Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.  It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.
List of video games that support cross-platform play | List of video games that support cross-platform play

Game engine | A game engine is a software framework designed for the creation and development of video games. Developers use them to create games for consoles, mobile devices and personal computers.  The core functionality typically provided by a game engine includes a rendering engine ("renderer") for 2D or 3D graphics, a physics engine or collision detection (and collision response), sound, scripting, animation, artificial intelligence, networking, streaming, memory management, threading, localization support, scene graph, and may include video support for cinematics.  The process of game development is often economized, in large part, by reusing/adapting the same game engine to create different games, or to make it easier to port games to multiple platforms.

StepMania | StepMania is a cross-platform rhythm video game and engine.  It was originally developed as a simulator of Konami's arcade game series "Dance Dance Revolution", and has since evolved into an extensible rhythm game engine capable of supporting a variety of rhythm-based game types.  Released under the MIT License, "StepMania" is open source free software.
ResidualVM | ResidualVM (formerly Residual) is a cross-platform computer program comprising 3D game engine recreations with a common graphical user interface.  It supports "Grim Fandango" and "".

Filmation (game engine) | Filmation is the trademark name of the isometric graphics engine employed in a series of games developed by Ultimate Play the Game during the 1980s, primarily on the 8-bit ZX Spectrum platform, but various titles also appeared on the BBC Micro, Amstrad CPC, MSX and Commodore 64 platforms.
hop2_titles: List of video games that support cross-platform play
response.summary_2: Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.
response.answer: If you mean the engine's technical/runtime setting (platforms): Snowdrop is for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One. If you mean the fictional setting of a game using Snowdrop (e.g., Tom Clancy's The Division), the provided summaries do not include that setting—please clarify which you want.
